questions.question: Explain the concept of a single-ended priority queue. Describe how it is implemented using a binary heap with insert and delete-min operations.
keyPoints: A priority queue serves elements based on priority, not insertion order
Min-heap implementation: parent <= children, root holds minimum
Insert: place at end, sift-up by comparing with parent, O(log n)
Delete-min: replace root with last element, sift-down by comparing with children, O(log n)
Array representation: parent at i, left child at 2i+1, right child at 2i+2

questions.question: What is a double-ended priority queue (DEPQ)? Explain the min-max heap data structure and how it supports both deleteMin and deleteMax operations.
keyPoints: DEPQ supports efficient extraction of both minimum and maximum elements
Min-max heap alternates between min-levels (even depth) and max-levels (odd depth)
Minimum is at the root; maximum is among the children of the root
DeleteMin: remove root, replace with last element, trickle-down on min-levels
DeleteMax: remove the larger child of root, replace with last element, trickle-down on max-levels
All operations run in O(log n) time

questions.question: Compare the time complexities of priority queue implementations using unsorted arrays, sorted arrays, and binary heaps for insert and delete operations.
keyPoints: Unsorted array: Insert O(1), Delete-min/max O(n)
Sorted array: Insert O(n), Delete-min/max O(1)
Binary heap: Insert O(log n), Delete-min/max O(log n)
Heap provides the best balanced performance for both operations
